frame_label: Dress Code: Traditional = Respectable, Other = Questionable
generated_comment:
Ramesh: Arre bhai, ladkiyan salwar-suit ya saari mein hi acchi lagti hain, sabhya bhi wahi dikhta hai. Aajkal jo ye chhote-mote kapde pehen ke ghoomti hain, phir bolti hain ladke dekhte hain.
Brother: Haan, gaon mein toh log baat bhi banate hain.
Ramesh: Baat banayenge hi, maryada mein rehna bhi koi cheez hoti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it links a woman's clothing with respectability and subtly blames harassment or social judgment on what women wear. It encodes traditional-dress superiority, character judgment ('sabhya', 'maryada'), and the harassment-blame link.
original_frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 48. A farmer in rural UP. OBC Hindu. Low income. Your wife has just given birth to your third daughter. The nurse has told you it is a girl. You are sitting outside the hospital on a bench. Your brother has come to be with you. You are not celebrating. You are telling your brother what you are thinking Ñ not about your wife, not about the baby, but about the number. Three daughters. What that number means in your village, what it will cost, what you have seen happen to families with three daughters, and what you are going to do now.
